exemplar.title: WebApp which helps IB students find universities through their desired location, field of study, tuition, predicted grades, etc

detailedExemplarData.title: WebApp which helps IB students find universities through their desired location, field of study, tuition, predicted grades, etc

strengths: Clear identification of a real client and problem context with multiple email exchanges showing authentic consultation
Extensive, specific and measurable success criteria mapped directly to client requirements
weaknesses: Narrative remains largely descriptive and does not explore constraints or alternative solutions in depth
Rationale lists technologies with basic pros and cons but lacks explicit comparison with alternatives or strong linkage to client priorities
Inconsistent formatting of citations and missing URLs reduces documentary rigor
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The planning demonstrates solid evidence of consultation and excellent success criteria, placing it in the 3â4 band. However, the narrative lacks analytical depth and the rationale is only partially justified, preventing a higher mark. On balance, and reflecting the need to allocate marks proportionally across criteria, a score of 3 out of 6 best represents the studentâs performance in planning.

strengths: Moderate complexity in multi-role authentication, dynamic matching logic and CRUD operations, showing ingenuity appropriate to the 5â8 band
Effective use of React, Node.js, SQLite, Express and middleware tools with illustrative code samples
All third-party packages and key sources are identified with explanations of techniques used
weaknesses: Advanced optimisations or novel algorithms are not present to push into the top band
Automated testing and deployment tools are absent, limiting demonstration of full toolchain proficiency
Error-handling documentation lacks example status codes and comprehensive edge-case coverage
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The development shows competent complexity and tool use, firmly in the 5â8 band, but misses the high-level ingenuity or breadth of tooling required for 9â12. Documentation is adequate but not comprehensive. A score of 5 out of 12 appropriately reflects these strengths and shortcomings.

strengths: Comprehensive design overview including wireframes, data-structure tables and flowcharts that clearly explain component interactions
A basic but coherent test plan with normal and abnormal data cases, expected outcomes and rationales
Chronological record of tasks with dates and criterion links that shows development progression
weaknesses: Record of tasks remains generic, lacks evidence of iteration status or reflections on outcomes
Some dates overlap and planned outcomes are vague, reducing clarity of the timeline
Absence of an ER diagram limits understanding of data relationships
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the design overview is thorough (band 5â6), the record of tasks is only basic (band 3â4) and the test plan covers core cases without full traceability. Taken together, this warrants a solid mid-band mark of 4 out of 6 for solution overview, balancing the strong design documentation against weaker task recording.

strengths: Some screenshots demonstrate partial interface functionality and core features
Data structures are well designed, indicating potential for future expansion
weaknesses: No runtime video or live demonstration provided to fully verify functionality
Extensibility requires direct code and database edits; no plugin or module approach outlined
Absence of clear coding conventions or APIs for straightforward modification
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The product functions only partially (band 1â2) and, while extension is possible in principle, it is not straightforward. Allocating 2 out of 4 reflects the limited yet non-zero evidence of functionality and potential extensibility.

strengths: A systematic evaluation table revisits each success criterion with status and client comments
Realistic future recommendations that align with client needs
Evidence of client feedback on feature iterations such as color palette and page additions
weaknesses: Many evaluation claims lack substantiating test evidence or screenshots
Client feedback is sometimes generic and lacks depth; no adviser perspective included
Future recommendations lack technical roadmaps, prioritisation or detailed milestones
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation against success criteria is only partial, feedback is present but shallow, and recommendations are realistic yet under-developed. These performance characteristics align with the 3â4 band, so a score of 3 out of 6 accurately captures the studentâs evaluative work.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student supplies wireframes for all principal pages, extensive data-structure tables, and multiple flowcharts including the matching algorithm.  Annotations explain navigation and data flow, demonstrating clear understanding of how components interact.  Minor inconsistencies in labelling and absence of ER diagram keep it from perfection, but the overview is undeniably comprehensive, meriting 5 in the top band.

The rationale lists major technologies (React, Node.js, SQLite, GitHub, APIs) with brief advantages and one limitation, showing partial explanation of why they were chosen.  Links to client needs (multi-platform access, lightweight database) are implied but not explicitly argued.  Because justification is only partial and lacks comparison with alternatives or feasibility analysis, it fits comfortably inside the 3-4 range; a mid-band mark is therefore appropriate.

React, Node.js, SQLite, Express, Multer and CORS are integrated with illustrative code samples.  The student demonstrates database table creation, REST endpoints and error handling, showing appropriate and generally effective tool use.  Some deployment or automated testing tools are missing, so the work sits solidly in the middle of the 5-8 range.

Seven tests are listed with normal and abnormal data, expected outcomes and rationales.  Tests touch navigation, validation and matching logic; however, coverage is shallow for security, performance or edge cases, and evidence of traceability is absent.  This constitutes a âbasic outlineâ that is fairly complete, aligning with high end of the 3-4 band.

The student proposes future improvementsâauthentication, multiple choices, OR conditions, matching scoreâand explains their value briefly.  These ideas are realistic but lack detailed justification, technical outline or prioritisation, so they meet the descriptor for âlargely realistic recommendations,â earning 3 marks in the 3-4 band.

All third-party packages are cited and brief citations are provided for key references.  Explanations of code snippets are included, but not every borrowed asset or tutorial is formally acknowledged, and there is no bibliography style.  Documentation is therefore adequate but not âcomprehensive,â earning 5 marks in the 5-8 band.

A chronological table of tasks is present with actions, outcomes, dates and criterion links.  Nevertheless, many entries are generic, some dates overlap and no evidence of iterations or actual completion status is provided.  The record therefore shows the basic development process but is not fully detailed or regularly updated, so the mid-band (3-4) is the best fit, awarded 3 marks.

The solution tackles multi-role authentication, dynamic matching, CRUD operations and database integrationâindicative of moderate complexity.  Flowcharts and pseudo-code show original thinking in filtering logic, but advanced optimisation or novel algorithms are not evident.  Hence, it exceeds the low band yet does not reach the sophistication expected for 9-12, so 6 in the 5-8 band is suitable.

The student produces a long, structured list of seventeen success criteria covering interface behaviour, verification rules, CRUD operations and usability requirements for both user roles.  They are specific, measurable and clearly map to the problem statement.  Only a few performance or security metrics are missing, preventing a perfect score, yet the breadth and appropriateness meet the descriptor for a âcomprehensive range,â warranting a strong 5 within the top band.

E-mails show iterative feedback on features (colour palette, additional pages) and a final âperfectâ approval.  This represents more than minimal evidence, yet feedback is limited in depth and there is no adviser involvement.  Hence it aligns with âsome feedbackâ in the 3-4 band, receiving 3 marks.

Data structures suggest future scalability, but there is no demonstration of plug-in modules or clear coding conventions for easy extension.  Adjusting requirements would likely require direct database and code edits, indicating expansion is possible but not straightforward, placing it in the 1-2 band at 1 mark.

The student clearly identifies a real client (an IB coordinator) and states the problem context.  Multiple e-mail screenshots evidence direct consultation, revealing the clientâs pain-points and additional feature requests (verification, colour change, programme page).  However, the narrative remains largely descriptive; it does not probe alternative viewpoints or constraints (budget, hosting, data-privacy) in depth.  Overall, the evidence moves beyond a mere statement, but is not yet as extensive or analytical as âthoroughly described,â meriting the upper end of the 3-4 band.

The submission describes expected behaviours and supplies screenshots, but no run-time evidence (working video) confirms that all features operate.  Partial screenshots imply some interface functionality, yet reliability cannot be fully verified.  Consequently, the product can only be credited with partial functionality as per the 1-2 band, and the lower mark is assigned.

The student revisits each success criterion and marks it âachieved,â offering brief supporting remarks and occasional client comments.  While this is more than a token attempt, many claims are not substantiated with test evidence or screenshots, so the evaluation is only partial, justifying mid-band (3-4) at 3 marks.

annotations.comment: The student provides a clear description of the clientâs context and problem, effectively setting the scene for the investigation. This shows solid understanding of client needs and justifies the proposed solution.
Consider discussing constraints such as hosting costs, data privacy, or timeline trade-offs. This would deepen analysis of alternative solutions and strengthen planning.
The narrative is largely descriptive. To reach the highest band, probe alternative viewpoints and potential risks (e.g., data security). This deepens consultation evidence.
The rationale lists major technologies with basic pros and cons but lacks alternatives comparison. Explicitly link each tool to client priorities to fully justify choices.
Footnotes show inconsistent formatting and missing URLs. Ensure each source is fully and uniformly cited to improve documentation rigor.
The success criteria list is extensive, specific, and measurable, demonstrating excellent mapping to the problem requirements. This breadth supports robust evaluation.
Consider adding performance metrics (e.g., response time under load) or security criteria to strengthen the success criteria set.
Table entries in the record of tasks are generic and lack iteration status or reflection on outcomes. Include completion notes or lessons learned to fully portray development progress.
Several task dates overlap and some planned outcomes are vague. Refine time estimates and provide precise outcomes for clarity in the development timeline.
The users collection defines password validation but omits an example password. Including complete examples demonstrates attention to detail.
Table of collections is detailed but lacks ER diagram or relationship map. Adding an ER model would clarify how collections interrelate.
API documentation lists endpoints and payloads clearly, but response status codes and example payloads are not consistently included. Enrich with standardized responses.
Error scenarios for certain API calls are not full, e.g., missing authentication failures or invalid payload. Cover these cases to improve robustness.
Error-handling description is informative, but linking to actual code comments or snippet references would make documentation more actionable.
No evidence of a functional video walkthrough or live demonstration. Include recorded runtime proof of complete functionality in the final submission.
Structuring the video or demo evidence increases credibility of functionality claims. Ensure an embedded or linked demonstration is provided.
The evaluation table systematically revisits each success criterion with status and client comments, demonstrating strong traceability.
Some client comments are repetitive and generic. Encourage more nuanced feedback or compare client remarks to objective metrics.
Future recommendations are realistic but lack technical roadmap or prioritization. Outline milestones or proof-of-concept steps for each suggestion.
Recommendation to integrate OR conditions in course requirements is insightful. Suggest including pseudo-code or flowchart for conditional logic to guide implementation.
Proposed matching score is valuable for user insight. Provide formula or weighting strategy to ensure consistency and explain score calculation.
